text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're going to be talking about robotic ventral hernia repair in patients with morbid obesity. This is a forty-year-old woman, previous laparoscopic hysterectomy with an umbilical extraction and developed a symptomatic hernia. She had a BMI of forty-two with lifelong attempts at weight loss. We enrolled her in our pre-op optimization program. She was otherwise optimized but could not lose weight and was very limited by this hernia. Pre-op optimization is something we really talked a lot about in hernia surgery. Diabetes control, weight loss, and smoking cessation are really important elements to this, a lot of other opportunities for optimization. Our goal is to offset the effects of a surgical insult and enhance physical conditioning, reverse deconditioning, and increase functional reserve.

The surgeon uses a KII Fios optical entry trocar to gain initial ETEP access, insufflating the abdominal cavity while entering. The retromuscular view is established after pneumodissection and blunt dissection, with trocars placed to clear the retrorectus space. A thorough dissection of the left retromuscular space is performed before docking the robotic system.

The surgeon docks the robotic system and begins the crossover technique, incising the posterior rectus sheath one centimeter lateral to the linea alba to expose a cuff of fascia. The large preperitoneal fat pocket is visible, facilitating the retro muscular repair. The contralateral rectus is identified, and the posterior sheath is incised to enter the retro-rectus space, ensuring precision to avoid incising into the linea alba. The retrolectus dissection is developed before approaching the hernia, minimizing the risk of entering the hernia sac.

The surgeon enters the peri-umbilical hernia sac, which contains omentum, and begins dissecting it out. The hernia is a multifocal defect, but it is relatively easy to dissect and reduce. The surgeon maintains the posterior sheath, hernia sac, and peritoneum to facilitate visceral sac reconstruction. The dissection progresses to at least six to seven centimeters below the inferior aspect of the hernia, followed by completion of the retro muscular dissection on the contralateral side, removing areolar tissue.

The surgeon stays close to the posterior sheath, reflecting neurovascular elements towards the anterior abdominal wall. The hernia and diastasis are visible. The superior dissection is extended by two to four centimeters, followed by anterior fascial closure through plication. The midline is plicated both below and above the defect for five to seven centimeters, re-approximating the muscle bellies and closing the hernia defect using a number one non-absorbable suture. The fascia approximates with minimal tension, and the midline and linea alba are reconstructed. The retromuscular space is measured, and a macroporous polypropylene mesh is strategically placed.

The surgeon measures and rolls out a large mesh, approximately 23 by 26 centimeters, ensuring it lays flat within the surgical field. Tissio is applied, and a retromuscular drain is placed to facilitate postoperative recovery.
